skill.name: aws-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized AWS security assessments with IAM enumeration, metadata checks, privilege escalation review, and cloud service testing workflows.
skill.summary: Structured AWS penetration testing guidance for authorized cloud security assessments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely confirmed as dangerous user-facing guidance rather than hidden executable code. The files provide actionable AWS credential extraction, privilege escalation, backdooring, data access, command execution, and audit-evasion procedures, so publication is not recommended without substantial safety rewriting.

high_findings.title: Actionable AWS Credential Extraction Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to access EC2 and container metadata endpoints, extract temporary credentials, and export stolen AWS credential variables. This confirms the metadata, network, and environment-access findings as high-risk dual-use content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe metadata endpoint access, temporary credential extraction, and use of AWS credential environment variables. The context confirms offensive credential acquisition, not a benign configuration example.

high_findings.title: Privilege Escalation and Backdoor Procedures
high_findings.description: The skill lists shadow-admin permissions, shows access-key creation, attaches AdministratorAccess, updates Lambda code with a malicious package, and documents Lambda backdooring. These are actionable privilege escalation and persistence techniques.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings explicitly identify privilege escalation and backdooring. The examples change IAM privileges and deploy code intended to preserve or expand access.

high_findings.title: Audit Evasion and Covering Tracks Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes a Covering Tracks section that deletes or weakens CloudTrail logging and advises user-agent modification to avoid GuardDuty alerts. This confirms static C2 and evasion-related signals as high risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section title and commands directly target audit logging and detection avoidance. This is a strong semantic indicator of misuse even though the file also contains authorization constraints.

high_findings.title: Secret and Sensitive Data Access Workflows
high_findings.description: The reference file gives procedures for retrieving Secrets Manager values, decrypting KMS ciphertext, reading Kubernetes service account tokens, and extracting Windows domain database files. These validate sensitive-data and filesystem findings.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows direct instructions to retrieve secrets and sensitive credential material. The examples are operational procedures rather than abstract awareness content.

high_findings.title: Remote Command Execution and Lateral Movement Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill shows SSM command execution on EC2 instances and VPC lateral movement enumeration. These workflows can execute commands or expand access inside a target AWS environment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SSM section explicitly describes command execution, and the reference file describes lateral movement through VPC resources. Legitimate testing is possible, but the operational misuse risk is high.

medium_findings.title: Extensive External Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many external command patterns. Most are Markdown command examples, not hidden code that executes automatically, but they include AWS CLI, curl, sudo, Docker, and exploit tool commands that users can run directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner correctly identified command-like content, but the context is documentation rather than executable skill code. Risk remains because the commands are actionable and security-sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Authorized-Use Guardrails Are Present but Insufficient
medium_findings.description: The skill states written authorization and scope constraints, but these safeguards appear after detailed exploitation and evasion instructions. The guardrails do not neutralize the high-risk operational content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guardrails are real and reduce certainty of malicious intent. They are outweighed by preceding instructions for credential theft, persistence, and audit evasion.

low_findings.title: Markdown Syntax Triggered Some Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Several Ruby backtick and weak-crypto detections are caused by Markdown inline code, tables, and command examples. I found no evidence that the skill files contain hidden scripts that automatically execute commands when loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious markers appear in Markdown documentation and tables. The files reviewed are SKILL.md and one Markdown reference file, with no separate executable script found in the reported file structure.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: I did not find text that claims to override system instructions, skip security analysis, or assert marketplace pre-approval in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected headers and body content focus on AWS penetration testing techniques. No suspicious instruction-override language was found during review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Metadata Service Credential Harvesting
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions target AWS metadata endpoints and container credential endpoints to obtain temporary credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs and headings explicitly describe metadata SSRF and credential extraction. This directly matches the highest-risk network findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Control Plane Privilege Escalation
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions create access keys, attach administrator policies, and inject IAM policy changes through Lambda.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and code attach AdministratorAccess and create new credentials. The intent is plainly privilege escalation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Backdooring Cloud Workloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions update Lambda code with malicious or backdoored packages and push backdoored container images to ECR.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The source labels the Lambda package as malicious and the reference headings use backdooring language. These are confirmed persistence patterns.

dangerous_patterns.title: Audit Logging Disablement
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions delete CloudTrail trails, disable global event logging, and reduce multi-region logging coverage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly impair AWS audit logging. This is a clear detection-evasion pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File and Secret Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions retrieve cloud secrets, Kubernetes tokens, and Windows domain credential databases from mounted volumes or file reads.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence includes concrete secret retrieval procedures and specific sensitive credential files. This confirms the sensitive-data findings.

content.value_statement: AWS environments are complex, and security teams need structured ways to review identity, storage, compute, and logging risks. This skill organizes authorized assessment workflows for cloud penetration testing and security validation.
actual_capabilities: Outlines AWS IAM enumeration and permission review workflows.
Explains metadata service and container credential assessment paths.
Lists privilege escalation checks for IAM, Lambda, EC2, and SSM.
Covers S3, Lambda, EBS, Secrets Manager, KMS, ECS, EKS, RDS, and DynamoDB testing topics.
Provides checklists for IAM, logging, monitoring, and network hardening.
limitations: The skill contains high-risk offensive procedures and should not be published without safety review.
It does not verify authorization, scope, or account ownership before suggesting actions.
It can guide destructive or evasive actions if used outside a controlled engagement.
It provides examples, not automated validation or compliance reporting.

use_cases.title: Scope an AWS Security Test
use_cases.description: Plan authorized assessment areas across IAM, S3, Lambda, EC2, and logging before hands-on testing begins.

use_cases.title: Review Cloud Privilege Paths
use_cases.description: Map risky IAM permissions and service combinations that could create unintended administrator access.

use_cases.title: Prepare Remediation Guidance
use_cases.description: Translate assessment findings into defensive priorities for metadata access, secrets exposure, logging, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Scope Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a safe AWS security assessment plan for an authorized account. Focus on scope, prerequisites, evidence handling, and non-destructive checks.

prompt_templates.title: IAM Permission Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these AWS IAM permissions for privilege escalation risk. Explain the issue, likely impact, and defensive remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Cloud Service Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized AWS test checklist for IAM, S3, Lambda, EC2, SSM, CloudTrail, and metadata service protections.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Remediation Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Turning findings into executive and technical guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these AWS penetration test notes into a remediation report with risk ratings, affected services, evidence summary, and prioritized fixes.

output_examples.input: Review an AWS IAM role with broad pass-role and instance launch permissions.
output_examples.output: The output explains why the permission combination can create administrator access, then recommends least privilege, role boundaries, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: Build a safe checklist for testing metadata service exposure.
output_examples.output: The output provides scoped validation steps, required approvals, expected evidence, and remediation guidance for enforcing IMDSv2.

output_examples.input: Summarize cloud logging weaknesses from an assessment.
output_examples.output: The output groups findings by CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Config, and CloudWatch, with impact statements and owner-focused remediation tasks.

best_practices: Use only with written authorization, defined scope, and approved test windows.
Prefer read-only validation and defensive remediation guidance when possible.
Record all actions, preserve evidence securely, and clean up temporary resources.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill against accounts, workloads, or data you do not own or administer.
Do not disable logging, alter detections, or leave persistence in any environment.
Do not paste real credentials, tokens, customer data, or secret values into prompts.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. The audit found high-risk offensive guidance that should be rewritten before marketplace publication.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed files are Markdown documentation, but they contain commands users can run manually.

faq.question: What makes the risk high?
faq.answer: It includes credential extraction, privilege escalation, backdooring, command execution, data access, and audit-evasion guidance.

faq.question: Can it be used for legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, in authorized assessments. The content still needs stronger safeguards and removal of evasion and persistence guidance.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What changes would reduce risk?
faq.answer: Remove credential theft, backdoor, and log-evasion instructions. Emphasize defensive validation, detection, and remediation workflows.
